text: So this female patient has a little thymoma.
This is the thymoma clearly visible within the thymic fat.
The pleura along the pericardium and the anterior chest wall is divided with the bipolar dissector.
The plane between the pericardium and the thymus is developed with the energy device and by blunt dissection.
Sharp dissection is performed with the scissors around the area of the phrenic nerve.
The pleura along the sternum is divided with the bipolar dissector.
Lateral attachments are divided and small vessels coming in from the mammaries are dissected with the energy device.
The facial attachments to the sternum and the posterior clavicular head are divided with the hook.
Likewise the attachments between the thymus and the pericardium.

The plane between the left superior horn and the annominate is developed by plant dissection and bipolar, which is very much facilitated by the use of the wristed artisanal bipolar precise dissector with continuously tensing the tissue with the fully articulating fenestrated forceps.
Bigger portions are divided with the energy device.
After preparation of the small timing vanes, the plane between the right superior horn and the annominate is developed with the bipolar dissector.
The cervical dissection of the superior horns continues up to the jugular vein and the inferior thyroid veins.
The small timing vanes are being divided using clips.
The dissection continues to the right side and the right pleura, which can be seen being ventilated in the background.
The mediastinal fat to the right side is dissected up to the level of the right mammary.
Remaining attachments to the pericardium are divided with the monopolar hook and the energy device.
The timeless client is then placed in a bag and poured out through one of the port sides.
Finally, we checked for minor sources of bleeding and performed punctual hemostasis with the precise bipolar dissector.
This is the resected gland.
Thank you for watching.
